DISCOVERY 302, PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S MOTION FOR ORDER COMPELLING FURTHER RESPONSES TO DEMAND FOR PRODUCTION: THE MOTION TO COMPEL FURTHER RESPONSES TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION SET TWO IS GRANTED AS FOLLOWS AND OTHERWISE DENIED: DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC.'S PRELIMINARY STATEMENT AND GENERAL OBJECTIONS ARE STRICKEN AS BOILERPLATE. SEE KOREA DATA SYS. CO. V. SUPERIOR COURT, 51 CAL.APP.4TH 1513, 1516 (1997). DEFENDANT HAS NOT IDENTIFIED ANY TRADE OR CONFIDENTIAL INFORMATION AND ITS OBJECTIONS THEREON ARE OVERRULED. DEFENDANT'S OBJECTIONS ON GROUNDS OF VAGUENESS, MATTERS OF FORM AND LACK OF RELEVANCE ARE UNSUPPORTED AND ARE OVERRULED. DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC. SHALL SERVE CODE-COMPLIANT RESPONSES TO REQUESTS FOR PRODUCTION NOS. 1-5 AND SHALL PRODUCE ALL RESPONSIVE DOCUMENTS (AND NOT SUMMARIES THEREOF) WITHIN 21 DAYS OF THE DATE OF ORDER HEREON WITH THE EXCEPTION THAT DEFENDANT'S PRIVACY OBJECTION IS SUSTAINED AS TO EMPLOYEE PERSONAL INFORMATION CONCERNING MEDICAL LEAVE, WORKERS COMPENSATION AND FINANCIAL MATTERS WHICH MAY BE REDACTED. THE MOTION IS DENIED AS TO REQUEST NO. 6 ON GROUNDS IT DOES NOT SEEK THE PRODUCTION OF ANY EXISTING DOCUMENT OR OTHER THING, BUT INSTEAD TO COMPEL THE CREATION OF A PRIVILEGE LOG. IN THAT CONNECTION, IF DEFENDANT BASES AN OBJECTION ON A CLAIM OF PRIVILEGE OR A CLAIM THAT THE INFORMATION SOUGHT IS PROTECTED WORK PRODUCT, DEFENDANT SHALL COMPLY WITH CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 2031.240(C)(1). SANCTIONS ARE AWARDED TO PLAINTIFF AND AGAINST DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC. IN THE AMOUNT OF $2.20, PAYABLE WITHIN TEN DAYS AFTER THE DATE OF ORDER. JPT TO REVIEW PROPOSED ORDER SUBMITTED BY PLAINTIFF AND MAKE CHANGES AS NEEDED TO REFLECT WHAT WAS DISCUSSED. JUDGE PRO TEM: JAMES FLEMING, CLERK: S. LE, NOT REPORTED. =(302/JPT)

DISCOVERY, DEPT. 302, AS TO THE SEP-8-2020 HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S MOTION FOR ORDER EXTENDING TIME FOR SERVICE OF RESPONSE TO INTERROGATORIES, THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION TO EXTEND HIS TIME TO OCTOBER 31, 2020 RESPOND TO DEFENDANT'S FIRST SET OF FORM INTERROGATORIES IS DENIED. PLAINTIFF HAS MADE NO SHOWING THAT WOULD JUSTIFY A RIGHT TO HAVE IN EXCESS OF 90 DAYS TO RESPOND. THE COURT WILL ENTER AN ORDER GRANTING AN EXTENSION TO SEPTEMBER 30, 2020. WITH THE ADMONITION TO PLAINTIFF THAT IF HE FAILS TO SERVE DEFENDANT WITH FULL AND COMPLETE RESPONSES BY SEPTEMBER 30, THE COURT WILL CONSIDER IMPOSING SANCTIONS AGAINT HIM. THE COURT REQUESTS THAT COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANT PREPARE AN APPROPRIATE ORDER, SEND A COPY TO PLAINTIFF FOR APPROVAL AS TO FORM AND SEND A COPY TO THE COURT FOR SIGNATURE AND FILING. JUDGE PRO TEM: PAUL RENNE; CLERK: SEAN KANE; COURT REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR NO. 8600 MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM. (302/JPT)

LAW AND MOTION 302, JUL-26-2023. HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO, JR.'S MOTION TO AMEND SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT AGAINST COMCAST TO JOIN NEW PARTY DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO. NOT ARGUED/CONTESTED. THE COURT ADOPTS ITS TENTATIVE RULING AS FOLLOWS: PLAINTIFF'S MOTION "TO AMEND SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT AGAINST COMCAST TO JOIN NEW PARTY DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO" IS DENIED. PRO PER PLAINTIFF HAS BEEN ADJUDGED A VEXATIOUS LITIGANT SUBJECT TO A CCP 391.7 PREFILING ORDER THAT BARS HIM FROM "FILING ANY NEW LITIGATION" WITHOUT "FIRST OBTAINING LEAVE OF THE PRESIDING JUDGE." (ID.) "LITIGATION" BROADLY INCLUDES A "CIVIL ACTION OR PROCEEDING" AGAINST WHAT PLAINTIFF HERE CONCEDES IS A "NEW PARTY," AND PLAINTIFF HAS NOT OBTAINED THE REQUIRED LEAVE. (CCP 391(A).) IN ANY EVENT, PLAINTIFF'S MOTION IS CLEARLY VEXATIOUS AND UNTIMELY AS THE CITY WAS GRANTED JUDGMENT ON THE PLEADINGS AND DEMURRERS IN THIS CASE YEARS AGO. SEE MINI-MINUTES FOR THIS DATE. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR. CLERK: ROBERT WOODS. NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU) ***AFTER HEARING, AN ORDER IS SIGNED. (SEE SCANNED ORDER FOR DETAILS)

calendar.matters:
Notice Of And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MYDATT SERVICES, INC.'s Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.'s motion to compel further responses to Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests for Production of Documents, Set 1 is GRANTED.

The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Mydatt has shown Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete. In these responses, DiGiacomo merely provides his objections and states he will nonetheless "comply with the demand" by listing documents to requests for which no objection is being made. As DiGiacomo objects to every response, this statement essentially means he will not identify any documents. Moreover, Mydatt represents that DiGiacomo has only provided one document, a fee waiver, in response to these requests. DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete.

Next, DiGiacomo has not demonstrated that any of his objections have merit. He claims his sole asset is exempt as Supplemental Security Income (SSI), thus these requests are not likely to lead to discovery of admissible evidence. But whether his assets are ultimately exempt from levy is distinct from whether the requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court orders DiGiacomo to provide further responses to "post-judgment discovery, requests for production of documents" set 1 Nos. 1 through 62.
Mydatt's meet and confer was reasonable. The court does not find DiGiacomo acted with substantial justification. DiGiacomo shall pay $1,125 to Mydatt in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Joinder And Joinder In Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC's Joinder In Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.'s Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Requests For Production Of Documents.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Recology, Inc.'s joinder to Mydatt's motion to compel further responses to post-judgment requests for production of documents, set 1, is denied for failure to provide a meet and confer declaration, as required by CCP 2031.310(b)(2). The court finds that neither party acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of And Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT MYDATT SERVICES, INC's Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories; Request For Sanctions.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.'s motion to compel further responses to post-judgment special interrogatories, set 1 is GRANTED.

The requests at issue are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. (CCP Section 2017.010.) Mydatt has shown Plaintiff Raymond DiGiacomo's responses are incomplete. For instance, special interrogatories No. 2 asks DiGiacomo to state the amount of his rent. DiGiacomo's response, i.e., that his sole assets are currently limited to disability payments, does not answer this question.

Next, DiGiacomo has not demonstrated that any of his objections have merit. He claims his sole asset is exempt as Supplemental Security Income (SSI), thus no answers he provides would leave to discovery of admissible evidence. (Oppo, 4.) But whether his assets are ultimately exempt from levy is distinct from whether the requests are reasonably calculated to lead to the discovery of admissible evidence. The court orders DiGiacomo to provide further responses to "post-judgment discovery, special interrogatories" set 1 Nos. 1 through 25.

Mydatt's meet and confer was reasonable. The court does not find DiGiacomo acted with substantial justification. DiGiacomo shall pay $1,575 to Mydatt in sanctions, payment no later than 10 court days from notice of this order.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

Notice Of Joinder And Joinder In Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc.S Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories
Off Calendar Jan-31-2025 Denied
MINI MINUTES:

Hearing Re: DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC.'s Joinder In Defendant Mydatt Services, Inc's Motion To Compel Plaintiff To Further Respond To Post-Judgment Discovery, Special Interrogatories.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Defendant Recology, Inc.'s joinder to Mydatt's motion to compel further responses to post-judgment special interrogatories, set 1, is denied for failure to provide a meet and confer declaration, as required by CCP 2030.300(b). The court finds that neither party acted with substantial justification and does not award sanctions.

Order submitted.

Judge: Christine Van Aken; Clerk: V. Da Fonseca; Not reported. (301/CVA)

RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO, JR. Notice Of Motion And Motion For Sanctions Against Usbid, Usf, And Mydatt, Their Attorney Rosaline S. Ayoub, And Her Firm Ayoub Law, Apc; Declaration; And, Memorandum.

All parties stipulate to the Judge Pro Tem. The pro tem Judge and parties (all appearing remotely) authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Ruling is discussed. At conclusion of discussion, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling:

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND VINCENT DIGIACOMO, JR.'S (HEREAFTER EITHER "PLAINTIFF" OR "DIGIACOMO") MOTION FOR SANCTIONS PURSUAN TO SECTION/ 128.5, CALIF. CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE AGAINST DEFENDANTS UNION SQUARE BUSINESS IMPROVEMENT DISTRICT ("USBID"), MYDATT SERVICES INC. ("MYDATT"), ROSALINE S. AYOUB, ESQ. AND AYOUB LAW, APC (HEREAFTER COLLECTIVELY "DEFENDANTS") IS DENIED. THERE IS A SERIOUS QUESTION AS TO WHETHER THE ALLEGED MISCONDUCT BY DEFENDANTS FALLS WIHTIN THE PROVISIONS OF SECTION 128.5. IN ANY CASE THE RECORD REFLECTS THAT THE WITNESS STATEMENTS FILED BY THE SUBPEONAED WITNESSES AND DEFENDANTS WERE IN FULL CONPLIANCE WITH THE COURT'S ORDER OF JULY 28, 2022. THE COURT'S ORDER MAKES CLEAR THAT THE ONLY PURPOSE OF THESE STATEMENTS WAS TO ELIMINATE ANY OBJECTIONS AS TO PERSONAL SERVICE. THERE WAS NO DUTY ON DEFENDANTS OR THE SUBPOENAED WITNESS TO COMMIT TO ATTENDING THE TRIAL IN RESPONSE TO THE SUBPOENA OR WAIVE ANY OBJECTIONS THEY MIGHT OTHER SUFFICIENCY OF SERVICE.

THE COURT WILL IMPOSE NO MONETARY SANCTIONS AGAINST ANY PARTY. DEFENDANTS' REQUEST FOR SANCTIONS IN THE AMOUNT OF TEN THOUSAND, SIX HUNDRED AND TWENTY-FIVE DOLLARS ($10,625.00) IS UNREASONABLE IN AMOUNT AND UNJUSTIFIED.

COUNSEL FOR DEFENDANTS SHOULD PREPARE A PROPOSED ORDER CONSISTENT WITH THIS TENTATIVE RULING, SUBMIT A COPY TO DIGIACOMO FOR APPROVAL AS TO FORM AND DELIVER A COPY IN WORDFORM TO THE COURT (rennepar85@gmail.com) IN ADVANCE OF THE HEARING ON NOVEMBER 21, 2023.

Notice Of Motion And Motion For Preliminary Injunction Against Comcast; Declaration; And, Memorandum
Off Calendar Sep-01-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO, JR.'S Motion For Preliminary Injunction Against Comcast;

Denied.

The present motion is for a mandatory injunction issued to defendant Comcast to address the noise emanating from a vault cover near plaintiff's residence. Plaintiff alleges that when a vehicle runs over the vault cover, it make an extremely loud noise.
1. Comcast asserts that plaintiff is a vexatious litigant and has not posted the required security. If this is so, the court questions why a motion based on that failure has not been made. See CCP 391.4 (dismissal for failure to furnish security). This does not appear to be an issue the court can resolve in the context of this motion for an injunction.
2. There is a high burden in securing mandatory injunctions. "A preliminary mandatory injunction is rarely granted, and is subject to stricter review on appeal." City of Corona v. AMG Outdoor Advertising, Inc. (2016) 244 Cal.App.4th 291, 299 (internal quotes removed).
3. The court considers the multi-year delay here in seeking injunctive relief. Volpicelli v. Jared Sydney Torrance Memorial Hosp. (1980) 109 Cal.App.3d 242, 253.
4. Comcast's objections to the plaintiff's declaration asserting negligence are sustained, as these are legal conclusions.
5. Plaintiff is unlikely to succeed on the merits of his claims. There is no violation of the Police Code: plaintiff is extremely unlikely to prove that the vaults are "broadcasting equipment." Further, plaintiff is not likely to show that there is a private nuisance because of the failure to show such a nuisance under an objective standard. Plaintiff has stated in discovery he is uniquely susceptible to noise. Nor is it likely that a public nuisance will be established, for similar reasons. There is no substantial evidence of negligence.
6. There is no evidence that the specific repairs in the proposed order for preliminary injunction are reasonable or would address the perceived problem.
7. Due the high burden in securing a mandatory injunction, the delay in seeking relief, and the facts that (i) plaintiff is unlikely to prove his case on the merits, and (ii) the relief sought is not shown to be effective, the motion is denied.
b. Sanctions are denied. They are sought apparently on the understanding that the injunction sought is to have Comcast repair vaults throughout the United States, or city-wide. The court reads the papers as seeking on the repair of one vault.

Counsel for the prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling in compliance with CRC 3.1312.

Judge: Curtis Karnow, Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/CK)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Amend Second Amended Complaint Against Comcast To Join New Party Defendant City And County Of San Francisco; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
Off Calendar Jul-26-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances (via Zoom):

Frank La Fleur, Esq., (415) 554-3826, for the City and County of San Francisco, Non-Party.

Craig Rolfe, Esq., FREEMAN MATHIS & GARY, LLP, (916) 472-3300, for Defendant Comcast.

No other appearances.

RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO, JR.'s Motion To Amend Second Amended Complaint Against Comcast To Join New Party Defendant City And County Of San Francisco.

Not argued/contested. The court court acknowledges that this matter was continued from yesterday. The court notes that Plaintiff did not appear at yesterday's hearing, was provided notice of today's hearing and failed to appear.

The court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Plaintiff's motion "to amend second amended complaint against Comcast to join new party defendant City and County of San Francisco" is denied. Pro per plaintiff has been adjudged a vexatious litigant subject to a CCP 391.7 prefiling order that bars him from "filing any new litigation" without "first obtaining leave of the presiding judge." (Id.) "Litigation" broadly includes a "civil action or proceeding" against what plaintiff here concedes is a "new party," and plaintiff has not obtained the required leave. (CCP 391(a).) In any event, plaintiff's motion is clearly vexatious and untimely as the City was granted judgment on the pleadings and demurrers in this case years ago. See mini-minutes for this date.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)

Motion And Defendant Recology, Inc.'S Motion For Prefiling Order Regarding Vexatious Litigant Plaintiff Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. And To Require Plaintiff To Furnish Security; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities (Motions)
Off Calendar Jul-18-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: RE: Defendant Recology, Inc.'s Motion for Prefiling Order Regarding Vexatious Litigant Plaintiff Raymond Digiacomo, Jr. and to Require Plaintiff to Furnish Security.

Appearances (via Zoom):

Raymond Digiacomo, Jr., in propria persona, Plaintiff.

Richard McKie, Esq., MANNING & KASS, ELLROD, RAMIREZ, TRESTER LLP, (415) 217-6990, for Defendant.

Ruling - ARGUED. Counsel offers argument specifically regarding the court's tentative ruling as to denial for request of security. At the conclusion of oral argument and having reviewed the pleadings, the court adopts its tentative ruling as follows: Defendant's motion for a prefiling order and to furnish security is granted in part and denied and in part. Prefiling order: Plaintiff was found to be a vexatious litigant on June 9, 2023. (See McKie Dec., Ex.N.) Defendant's request for a pre-filing order is granted. Plaintiff may not file any new litigation in the courts of this state in propria persona without first obtaining leave of the presiding judge of the court where the litigation is proposed to be filed. (CCP 391.7(a).) The clerk of the court shall provide the Judicial Council a copy of the prefiling order issued pursuant to subdivision (a). Security: A defendant may move the court for an order requiring a vexatious litigant to furnish security under CCP 391.1(a). The motion for an order requiring the plaintiff to furnish security shall be based on the following: (1) upon the ground, and supported by a showing, that the plaintiff is a vexatious litigant; and (2) there is not a reasonable probability he will prevail in the litigation against the moving defendant. (CCP 391.1(a).) Defendant as the moving party bears the burden of proving that plaintiff has no reasonable likelihood of prevailing. (See Golin v. Allenby (2010) 190 Cal.App.4th 616, 640-641.) The court is not bound to assume the truthfulness of the vexatious litigant's complaint. It may weigh the evidence presented on a motion to post security (e.g., declarations by defendant vs. declarations by plaintiff) in determining whether there is a "reasonable probability" of any recovery. (Moran v. Murtaugh Miller Meyer & Nelson, LLP (2007) 40 Cal.App.4th 780, 784.) Here, plaintiff has already been declared vexatious, both by order of this court and as confirmed by the Vexatious Litigant List. (See McKie Dec., Ex.N.) Therefore, the court need make no further finding with regard to the first part of the test under CCP 391.1(a). As to the part two of the test, defendant asserts that plaintiff has no reasonable probability of prevailing on any of his claims against it. Upon review of the evidence for purposes of ruling on this motion, the court finds a reasonable probability of recovery in the litigation against Defendant. (CCP 391.1(a).) Specifically, on May 15, 2023, when denying defendant's motion for summary judgment, Judge Quinn found that while the trash collection generally is not prohibited by law, it may still constitute a private nuisance if it unreasonably interferes with another's enjoyment of life or property, such that it causes a substantial actual injury. (See DiGiacomo Decl., Ex 1.) Defendant has the burden and failed to offer evidence to demonstrate that its trash collection practices do not unreasonably interfere with plaintiff's enjoyment of life or property such that it causes a substantial actual injury. Rather, defendant points to the Court of Appeal's Jun-30-2022 opinion which affirmed the denial of plaintiff's November 2020 request for preliminary injunction, as sufficient evidence that plaintiff currently cannot prevail. (MPA, 13:4-14:17.) Defendant did not meet its burden and the motion for security is denied.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr. Clerk: Robert Woods. Not reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion For A Protective Order Preventing Pltf From Taking The Deposition Of San Francisco Board Of Supervisors Member Aaron D. Peskin
Off Calendar Mar-01-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by Zoom):
Frank La Fleur, Esq. (Deputy city attorney) 415-554-3826 for Moving party AARON PESKIN.
Court reporter: Tamara Willat, CSR #4609, 415-272-1753, tamarawillat@sbcglobal.net; not reported.***on standby***
No other Appearance.

RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court announces no one sent an email contesting the tentative ruling.The Court having reviewed the pleadings adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

OTHER AARON PESKIN'S Motion For A Protective Order Preventing Pltf From Taking The Deposition Of San Francisco Board Of Supervisors Member Aaron D. Peskin.

Aaron Peskin's motion for a protective order preventing his deposition is granted. Peskin is president of San Francisco's board of supervisors; as such, he is a high-ranking government official "not subject to deposition absent compelling reasons." (Westley v. Sup. Ct. (2004) 125 Cal.App.4th 907, 910-11.) This is particularly so where, as here, plaintiff has not shown that much of the information he seeks "is not available through any other source." (Id.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Motion For Order Compelling Comcast To Further Respond To Srogs2 And Imposing Monetary Sanction; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
Off Calendar Jan-06-2023 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO's Motion For Order Compelling Comcast To Further Respond To SROGS2 And Imposing Monetary Sanction.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to compel further response to special interrogatories is granted as follows and otherwise denied. Defendant Comcast's objections to Special Interrogatories 29, 30 and 31 except as to privilege are stricken as boilerplate. Defendant Comcast shall serve a further response to Special Interrogatory No. 29 identifying by name and address ALL suppliers or assemblers of cable boxes placed within the City and County of San Francisco during the period January 1, 2020, to the present. Identification of installers of cable boxes or of manufacturers or assemblers of any individual cable box or boxes is not required. This order requires Defendant Comcast to identify its suppliers and/or assemblers of cable boxes for the stated area and time period or to certify under oath that it has no record whatsoever of the identity of any relevant supplier and/or assembler and that no managerial or professional employee of its executive, purchasing, contracting and accounts payable departments has any knowledge whatsoever of any relevant supplier and/or assembler.

Sanctions are denied for lack of evidence as to any expenses incurred by plaintiff and in the interest of justice.

Order signed.

Judge Pro Tem: James Fleming; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)

Motion For A Protective Order Restricting The Discovery Of Defendant Comcast Of California Iii, Inc. And Imposing Monetary Sanction; Declaration; And, Memorandum.
Off Calendar Apr-29-2022 Continued To May-12-22 At 09:00 A.M. In Dept 302

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Exemplary/Punitive And Treble Damage Claims From Second Amended Complaint (Motions)
Off Calendar Apr-28-2022 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Ray DiGiacomo (415-595-4107; raydigiacomojr@gmail.com) Plaintiff in Propria Persona (audio only).
Craig J. Rolfe, Esq. (916-472-3306; craig.rolfe@fmglaw.com) of Freeman Mathis & Gary for Defendant, Comcast of California III, Inc.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT COMCAST OF CALIFORNIA III, INC.'S Motion To Strike Exemplary/Punitive And Treble Damage Claims From Second Amended Complaint.

Comcast of California III, Inc.'s "motion to strike exemplary/punitive and treble damage claims from second amended complaint" (SAC) is denied.

Comcast asserts that the SAC "pleads no facts whatsoever to support a claim for exemplary/punitive or treble damages." (Memo. 2:1-2; see also Not. 3:6-7.) To the contrary, the SAC's factual allegations are adequate for the pleading stage. Whether, as Comcast doubts, plaintiff will eventually be "able to succeed" on his punitive damages prayer is a question for another day. (Memo. 7:5-6.)

Plaintiff alleges, for example, that he and other mentally and physically disabled people live in a supportive single room occupancy (SRO) hotel; that Comcast has long failed to maintain its cabling vaults outside the SRO's windows such that the covers make "gunshot" noises "day and night" when run over by vehicles; that he and others thus suffer sleeplessness and exacerbation of their mental disabilities; that Comcast management knows about the gunshot noises but has done little to abate them; and that the vexation has been worsened by Covid-19 lockdowns. (SAC 4:6-7:19.)

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

Atty. CRAIG J, ROLFE, Esq. of Freeman Mathis & Gary, LLP (Tel: 916-472-3300) for Defendant Comcast of California III, Inc., incorrectly sued as Comcast Corporation;
No other appearances made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S MOTION TO CONSOLIDATE ACTIONS OF CASE NOS. CGC-20-584604, CGC-20-588592 WITH CASE NO. CGC-19-581651.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to consolidate cases CGC-19-581651, CGC-20-584604 and CGC-20-588592 for all purposes is granted, with the former designated the lead case. In each of the three cases, plaintiff claims defendants create a nuisance with night-time noises outside his apartment. Thus, common questions of law and fact exist. Inconvenience to defendants is outweighed by, inter alia, the economies of avoiding three trials heard by three juries before three judges. The cases are not complex and jurors would not be confused.

Order signed.

Hearing Re: Plaintiff Raymond Digiacomo'S Discovery Motions Previously Vacated Pending Informal Discovery Conference Held On March 2, 2021.
Off Calendar Jun-10-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Raymond DiGiacomo (415-595-4107; raydigiacomojr@gmail.com) Plaintiff in propria persona.
Rich McKie (415-217-6990; rxm@manningllp.com) of Manning & Kass....LLP for Defendant, Recology, Inc.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Hearing Re: Plaintiff Raymond Digiacomo'S Discovery Motions Previously Vacated Pending Informal Discovery Conference Held On March 2, 2021.

After the parties to this action had served numerous discovery requests and subpoenas, and had filed numerous motions, the Court appointed a judge pro tem to hold an informal discovery conference, which was held on March 2. Following that conference, the judge pro tem issued a lengthy report and accompanying proposed stipulation and order, which plaintiff refused to enter into. The Court hereby adopts the provisions of the recommended stipulation as its order, as follows:

1. Plaintiff's motions to quash subpoenas for business records addressed to UCSF Medical Center (radiology, medical, and billing records), Hyde Street Community Services, Inc., and SFGH Adult Urgent Care Center are granted in part and denied in part. Each subpoenaed entity shall produce responsive documents, limited to their care and treatment of Plaintiff DiGiacomo for the past five years for any of the following conditions: humiliation, mental anguish, emotional and physical distress, mental health deterioration due to increased paranoia, increased anxiety, persistent lack of adequate sleep, increased suicidal thoughts, and ear canal soreness and bleeding due to excessive ear plug usage.
2. Plaintiff's motion to quash the subpoena for business records directed to the Tenderloin Housing Clinic is denied.
3. Plaintiff's motions to quash the subpoenas for business records directed to Marathon Staffing and San Francisco Chamber Orchestra are denied, except as to tax records (W-2 forms).
4. Plaintiff's motion to compel further responses to Request for Production of Documents, Set No. 3, is denied.

In addition, in an exercise of the Court's broad discretion to limit the scope of discovery to the end that the burden, expense and intrusiveness of discovery do not outweigh the likelihood that the information will lead to the discovery of admissible evidence, as well as to restrict the frequency or extent of discovery to limit the expense and burdens of discovery on the parties and the court, the Court orders as follows: The parties shall meet and confer before serving any notice of taking deposition, written interrogatory, request for production of documents, notice of inspection or pretrial subpoena or motion to compel, limit or prohibit discovery.

Counsel for Defendant is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the Court's ruling as discussed during the hearing.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

DEMURRER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jan-13-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via ZOOM Video-conferencing:

Raymond Vincent DiGiacomo, In Propria Persona (Tel: 415-595-4107) appears as self-represented plaintiff/opposing party

Deputy City Attorney, Timothy J Fama (Email: tim.fama@sfcityatty.org) of the San Francisco City Attorney's Office (Tel: 415) 554-3921) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

On Calendar:
DEFENDANT CITY AND COUNTY OF SAN FRANCISCO'S DEMURRER to SECOND AMENDED COMPLAINT

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides on record as reflected in the notes of the Certified Shorthand Reporter.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling with modification.

Defendant City and County of San Francisco's demurrer to Plaintiff's Second Amended Complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Plaintiff has not filed an opposition to the demurrer. Instead, Plaintiff filed an untimely motion to strike the demurrer, which is off calendar because Plaintiff did not serve and file the motion to strike at least 16 court days before the hearing, as required by Code of Civil Procedure section 1005(b). That Plaintiff is self-represented does not excuse these procedural deficiencies. "[T]he rules of civil procedure must apply equally to parties represented by counsel and those who forgo attorney representation." (Rappleyea v. Campbell, 8 Cal.4th 975, 984-85 (1994); see also ViaView, Inc. v. Retzlaff (2016) 1 Cal.App.5th 198, 208 ["While a party may choose to act as his or her own attorney, '[s]uch a party is to be treated like any other party and is entitled to the same, but no greater consideration than other litigants and attorneys."].)

Even if the Court were to deem the motion to strike to constitute Plaintiff's opposition to the demurrer, it lacks merit. Plaintiff consented to the hearing date on the demurrer, and therefore is estopped from objecting to it. Further, Plaintiff's Second Amended Complaint states a single cause of action for private nuisance against Defendant Recology, Inc. and affiliated entities (Recology) arising out of noise generated by Recology's waste collection activities. It does not state facts sufficient to state a cause of action against the City based solely on its contracting relationship with Recology.

Written order is signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

DEMURRER to ANSWER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-31-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S DEMURRER TO ANSWER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff's demurrer to defendant's answer to the Second Amended Complaint is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. Defendant is given one last chance to plead ultimate facts to support apposite defenses. (See FPI Development, Inc. v. Nakashima (1991) 231 Cal.App.3d 367, 384 [defenses must be pleaded with same detail as claims in complaint].) Some of the defenses appear totally irrelevant to this case, e.g., assumption of the risk. Defendant's general denials do not support the affirmative defenses. Affirmative defenses must be specially pleaded as they inject a new matter into the case. (See Code Civ. Proc. sec. 431.30(b)(2); Walsh v. West Valley Mission Comm. Coll. Dist. (1998) 66 Cal.App.4th 1532, 1545-1546; Houk v. Williams Bros. (1943) 58 Cal.App.2d 573, 582.)

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER to 2ND Amended COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Dec-31-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF RAYMOND DIGIACOMO'S MOTION TO STRIKE ANSWER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling. Plaintiff's motion to strike is denied. The verification is not defective. "[T]he object of a verification is to assure good faith in the averments or statements of a party." (Frio v. Superior Crt. (1988) 203 Cal.App.3d 1480, 1498.) Generally, where there is no allegation of bad faith, a claim of inadequate verification is without merit. (See League of Women Voters v. Eu (1992) 7 Cal.App.4th 649, 656.) "The defect [improper verification] is one of form which does not affect the substance." (See Soltani-Rastegar v. Sup. Crt. (1989) 208 Cal.App.3d 424, 428.) Plaintiff's other hyper-critical attacks on the answer lack merit and the Court rejects them under Code of Civil Procedure section 452 [courts must liberally construe pleadings].

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported (302/EPS)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Jul-07-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances (by videoconference via Zoom):
Ray DiGiacomo, Esq. 415-595-4107 raydigiacomojr@gmail.com for Plaintiff.
Nicole Hoikka, Esq. 213-624-6900 neh@manningllp.com of Manning & Kass Ellrod Ramirez Trester LLP for Defendant.

RULING - A R G U E D. The Court shares its views. Defendant argues. Plaintiff inquires re: sanctions.(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC.'s DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling.

Defendant Recology, Inc.'s demurrer to plaintiff's first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled. A private nuisance claim requires proof of three elements: (1) an interference with the plaintiff's use and enjoyment of property; (2) that interference caused the plaintiff to suffer substantial actual damage; and (3) the interference was unreasonable. (Wilson v. Southern California Edison Co. (2018) 21 Cal.App.5th 786, 802, citing San Diego Gas & Electric Co. v. Superior Court (1996) 13 Cal.4th 893.) In the Court's prior March 10, 2020 Order, plaintiff was granted leave to amend his complaint to allege that an ordinary person would reasonably be annoyed or disturbed by Recology's conduct and that the seriousness of the harm outweighs the public benefit of Recology's conduct. The first two elements were sufficiently pled. (See, e.g., Mendez v. Rancho Valencia Resort Partners, LLC (2016) 3 Cal.App.5th 248, 264 ["It is clear that '[e]xcessive and inappropriate noise may under certain circumstances constitute an interference with the present enjoyment of land amounting to a nuisance.'"].)

Plaintiff has sufficiently amended his complaint. The FAC alleges an ordinary person would be reasonably annoyed by Recology's conduct because the continuous, significant noise levels and vibrations are during the late evening and morning hours, while people are trying to sleep. (See FAC 23-26.) Additionally, plaintiff alleges that Recology can continue to collect trash, recyclables, and compost by modifying its schedule, perhaps to earlier in the evening or between the hours of 7 a.m. and 9 a.m. (Id. 27(d), 28(a).) Whether a given invasion is unreasonable is "to be determined by the trier of fact in each case in light of all the circumstances of that case." (San Diego Gas & Electric Co., 13 Cal.4th at 939; accord, Mendez, 3 Cal.App.5th at 258-250, 263-264 ["the elements of substantial damage and unreasonableness necessary to making out a claim of private nuisance are questions of fact that are determined by considering all of the circumstances of the case."] [trial court entered judgment in favor of defendant at conclusion of bench trial on plaintiffs' action for private nuisance].)(as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript)

Order is sgned. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Anthony Vaughn, CSR# 6185, tonyvaughn@yahoo.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)

Off Calendar Apr-28-2020 Continued To Jul-01-20 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-10-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

Appearances:
Raymond Vincent DiGiacomo, plaintiff appearing on his own behalf;
Mark Hagopian, Esq. of MANNING & KASS, T:415.217.6990, for defendant/moving party.

RULING: The matter was passed to allow plaintiff time to read the tentative ruling. Matter was recalled. Plaintiff argues. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted its tentative ruling on DEFENDANT RECOLOGY, INC.'s MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT as follows:

Defendant Recology, Inc.'s motion to strike is granted as to paragraphs 22 and 35 of the complaint and the prayer for punitive damages, and is otherwise denied. Even if punitive damages could be recovered in a claim for private nuisance, the complaint does not allege facts sufficient to warrant the prayer. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate only if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.) Plaintiff's allegations regarding defendant's conduct do not rise to that level, and there is no reasonable probability that he can amend the complaint to do so.

Order signed.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
